name: Where AI fits your business
tagline: Stop asking 'should we use AI' and start asking 'which of our repeated paperwork should go first' — a plain test for spotting the work AI is genuinely good at, a four-area map of your business, and a way to score candidates so you pick ONE and start this week.
overview: This module answers the only question that matters at the start: where do I even begin? The honest answer is not 'everywhere' and it is not 'wait and see' — it is 'find your repetitive knowledge work and pick one piece.' Repetitive knowledge work is any task you do more than about once a week, that follows a rough pattern, where the raw material is words or numbers — emails, quotes, reports, listings, reviews, invoices, meeting notes. You will learn a simple test to spot it, walk the four areas of any business where it hides (getting customers, serving customers, running the place, the numbers), and score each candidate on three things a non-technical owner can judge without a data scientist: how often it happens, how painful it is, and how bad a wrong answer would be. That last one — mistake-cost — decides HOW you let AI help (draft something for a human to check, act only with your approval, or run fully on its own), not WHETHER you use it at all. You will also learn the anti-hype half: what NOT to start with, why you must fix a broken process before you automate it (automating chaos just gives you faster chaos), and why picking one task beats trying to transform everything. The thing you build here — your AI opportunity map — is the input to every later module, and the one task you circle becomes the workflow you automate in Module 04.
objectives: Apply the repetitive-knowledge-work test — done more than weekly, follows a rough pattern, made of words or numbers — to separate work AI is genuinely good at from work it is not.

Walk the four areas of any business (getting customers, serving customers, running the place, the numbers) and list real candidate tasks in each, using the recurring cast of a cafe, an agency, an e-commerce shop, and a consultancy.
Score each candidate on three owner-judgeable axes — how often (frequency), how painful (pain), how bad a wrong answer is (mistake-cost) — and rank them.

Distinguish a quick win you can start this month (drafting, summarising, first-pass answers with a human still checking) from a transformation that is a whole-year project (whole workflows running with checkpoints — Modules 04 and 05).
Use the mistake-cost axis to decide HOW to deploy AI on a task — draft-for-a-human, act-with-approval, or fully automatic — rather than treating it as a yes/no on AI itself.

Name what NOT to start with (expensive or legal decisions, twice-a-year tasks, anything you'd be embarrassed to have a customer discover was automated) and apply the fix-the-process-first rule before automating anything.

lessons.title: The test: repetitive knowledge work
lessons.objective: Use one simple test to tell, for any task in your business, whether it is the kind of work AI is genuinely good at.
lessons.body:
## Stop asking the wrong question

Most owners start with *"should we use AI?"* That question has no useful answer, because AI is not one thing you either adopt or don't — it is a helper that is brilliant at a narrow kind of work and useless (or worse) at the rest. The better question is: **"which of our repeated paperwork should go first?"** To answer it, you need a way to spot the work AI is genuinely good at. Here is the whole test, and it fits on a beer mat.

## The repetitive-knowledge-work test

A task is a strong candidate for AI help when it passes all three checks:

1. **You do it more than about once a week.** Frequency is what makes the effort of setting it up pay back. A task you do twice a year is not worth automating, however annoying it is.
2. **It follows a rough pattern.** Not a rigid form — a *shape*. "Read the supplier's email, pull out what they can deliver, put it in the order sheet" is a pattern. "Have a brilliant original idea for a rebrand" is not.
3. **The raw material is words or numbers.** Emails, quotes, reports, product listings, customer reviews, invoices, meeting notes, timesheets. This is called **knowledge work** — work whose input and output are information, not physical things. AI reads and writes words and numbers; that is its home turf.

Put the two ideas together and you get the name for what you are hunting: **repetitive knowledge work**. Done often, follows a pattern, made of words or numbers. When a task ticks all three, AI can usually take a real bite out of it. When it misses one or more, be sceptical.

## Why these three, and not 'is it important?'

Notice what is *not* on the list: how important the task is, how senior the person doing it is, or how much you'd love to be rid of it. Those feelings are real but they mislead you. A task can be agonising and important and still be a bad first candidate — because it happens rarely, or because there is no pattern, or because a wrong answer is catastrophic (we get to that in Lesson 3). The test keeps you honest by asking only about the *shape* of the work, not your feelings about it.

## Worked example: the cafe's Monday morning

Meet the first of our four recurring businesses: a **neighbourhood cafe**. Every week, four or five suppliers email the owner what they've got, at what price, in no consistent format — one sends a PDF, one writes it in the email body, one attaches a spreadsheet. The owner reads all of them and hand-types a single weekly order sheet. It takes forty minutes every Monday and she hates it.

Run the test:

- **More than weekly?** It is *exactly* weekly. Pass.
- **Follows a pattern?** Yes — every week it is "read supplier messages, pull out items and prices, put them in one sheet." The suppliers change their wording; the shape never does. Pass.
- **Words or numbers?** Entirely. Emails in, a sheet of items and prices out. Pass.

Three passes. This is textbook repetitive knowledge work, and it is a strong first candidate. Contrast it with a task the same owner also finds painful: deciding whether to sign a five-year lease on a second location. Important? Enormously. But it happens *once*, there is no repeatable pattern, and a wrong answer is ruinous. It fails the test on every axis. Same owner, same week, two painful tasks — one is a great place to start with AI and one is a terrible one. The test is what tells them apart.

## What this buys you

You now have a filter. Before you get excited about any AI idea, run it through the three checks. In the next lesson you'll point that filter at your whole business, one area at a time, so you don't miss the good candidates hiding in plain sight.

knowledgeCheck.stem: The 'repetitive knowledge work' test asks three things about a task. Which three?

options.text: Done more than about weekly, follows a rough pattern, and the raw material is words or numbers
Important to the business, done by a senior person, and disliked by whoever does it
Cheap to fix, low-risk, and already partly automated
Customer-facing, revenue-generating, and measurable

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The test is deliberately about the shape of the work: frequency (more than weekly), pattern (a rough repeatable shape), and material (words or numbers). Option b describes feelings and seniority, which mislead you — a task can be important and senior and still be a bad first candidate. Option c is about cost and risk, which matter later but aren't the spotting test. Option d lists desirable business traits but none of them is what makes a task suit AI.

knowledgeCheck.stem: The cafe owner finds two tasks painful: turning weekly supplier emails into an order sheet, and deciding whether to sign a five-year lease. Why is the first a strong AI candidate and the second a poor one?

options.text: The order sheet is frequent, patterned, and made of words and numbers; the lease decision happens once, has no repeatable pattern, and a wrong answer is ruinous
The order sheet is more important to the business than the lease decision
The lease decision involves money, and AI can never help with anything involving money
The order sheet is disliked more, and dislike is what qualifies a task for AI

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The order sheet passes all three checks (weekly, clear pattern, words and numbers) while the lease fails all three (one-off, no pattern, catastrophic if wrong) — that is the whole reason. Option b is backwards: the lease is arguably far more important, yet it's still the worse candidate, which is exactly the point that importance doesn't decide this. Option c overstates things — AI can help gather and summarise information for money decisions; the lease is a bad candidate because it's a one-off high-stakes judgement, not because money is involved. Option d repeats the trap that dislike qualifies a task; it doesn't.

lessons.title: The map: four areas, and scoring what you find
lessons.objective: Walk the four areas of any business to find candidate tasks, then score each on frequency, pain, and mistake-cost to rank them.
lessons.body:
## Four places to look

Good candidates hide in plain sight, so you need a route that visits every corner. Any business — a cafe, a twelve-person agency, a global e-commerce shop, a solo consultancy — can be walked in four areas. Go through them one at a time and list the repetitive knowledge work in each:

1. **Getting customers** — sales and marketing. Proposals, quotes, social posts, outreach emails, ad copy, follow-ups.
2. **Serving customers** — support and delivery. Answering "where's my order?", replying to reviews, onboarding messages, FAQ responses, appointment confirmations.
3. **Running the place** — operations and admin. Turning supplier emails into orders, scheduling, meeting notes into action lists, updating internal docs, chasing paperwork.
4. **The numbers** — finance and reporting. Summarising timesheets, drafting status updates, categorising expenses, first-pass invoice checks, weekly figures into a plain-English note.

One example from each of our recurring cast makes it concrete:

- **Getting customers** — the **marketing agency** drafts proposals and social posts, over and over, for every client.
- **Serving customers** — the **e-commerce shop** answers "where's my order?" all day and replies to product reviews.
- **Running the place** — the **cafe** turns that pile of supplier emails into a weekly order sheet.
- **The numbers** — the **consultancy** summarises everyone's timesheets into a Friday status note for clients.

Each of those passed the Lesson 1 test. Walking the four areas is just how you make sure you find them all instead of fixating on the first annoyance that comes to mind.

## Scoring: three axes an owner can actually judge

Once you have a list, you need to rank it — because you are going to start with exactly one. Score each candidate on three axes, each roughly low / medium / high. You do not need data or a consultant; you already know these answers.

- **How often (frequency).** Daily beats weekly beats monthly. More repetition means more time back and faster payback.
- **How painful (pain).** How much time, tedium, or delay does it cause today? High-pain tasks are the ones people put off, which creates backlogs.
- **How bad is a wrong answer (mistake-cost).** If AI got this wrong and nobody caught it, what happens? A clumsy social-post draft is a shrug. A wrong number in a client invoice is a phone call you don't want. A wrong answer on a legal or safety question can be genuinely damaging.

A great first candidate is **high frequency, high pain, and low mistake-cost** — it happens constantly, it hurts, and if the AI slips, the cost of catching it is trivial. The cafe's order sheet is close to ideal: weekly (high-ish frequency), genuinely painful (forty grim minutes), and low mistake-cost (the owner eyeballs the sheet before ordering, so a mistake is caught in seconds).

## Worked example: scoring the consultancy's Friday note

The **consultancy** produces a Friday status note by reading everyone's timesheets and writing a short client-friendly summary of what got done. Score it:

- **Frequency:** weekly, like clockwork. Medium-to-high — enough repetition to be worth it.
- **Pain:** high. It lands at 4pm on a Friday when everyone is fried, and it's fiddly to pull together.
- **Mistake-cost:** low-to-medium. It's a summary a partner reads and signs off before it goes to the client, so an error is caught in the review — as long as that review actually happens.

That profile — frequent, painful, and low-cost *because a human checks it* — is exactly what you want in a first project. Notice the mistake-cost was low *only because* there's a human check before it reaches the client. Take that check away and the score changes. That link between mistake-cost and how you deploy is the whole subject of the next lesson.

## What this buys you

You now have a ranked shortlist instead of a vague sense that "we should do something with AI." Circle the top two or three. In Lesson 3 you'll use the mistake-cost score to decide *how* to let AI help each one — and that decision is what makes the difference between a useful tool and a liability.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You're mapping candidates across the four areas. The e-commerce shop answering 'where's my order?' all day sits in which area?

options.text: Serving customers (support and delivery)
Getting customers (sales and marketing)
Running the place (operations and admin)
The numbers (finance and reporting)

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Answering order-status questions is support for an existing customer, so it's 'serving customers.' It isn't 'getting customers' — that's sales and marketing work like ads and proposals, not helping someone who has already bought. It isn't 'running the place,' which is internal operations like turning supplier emails into orders. And it isn't 'the numbers,' which is finance and reporting like timesheet summaries.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Using the three scoring axes, which candidate profile is the strongest place to start?

options.text: High frequency, high pain, low mistake-cost
Low frequency, high pain, high mistake-cost
High frequency, low pain, high mistake-cost
Low frequency, low pain, low mistake-cost

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The ideal first project happens a lot (payback), genuinely hurts (worth fixing), and is cheap to get wrong (safe to try) — high frequency, high pain, low mistake-cost. Option b is a poor start: rare tasks don't pay back the setup, and high mistake-cost makes a first project risky. Option c mixes a high mistake-cost (risky) with low pain (little reason to bother), so it's not where you'd begin. Option d is low-stakes but also low-frequency and low-pain, so there's little to gain — you wouldn't prioritise it.

lessons.title: How, not whether: quick wins, mistake-cost, and what to avoid
lessons.objective: Use the mistake-cost axis to decide how to deploy AI on a task, tell a quick win from a transformation, and know what not to start with.
lessons.body:
## Mistake-cost decides HOW, not WHETHER

Here is the idea that keeps small businesses out of trouble. A high mistake-cost does **not** mean "don't use AI here." It means "use AI here *carefully*." The mistake-cost axis chooses the **way** you deploy, along a ladder of trust:

- **Draft for a human.** AI produces a first pass; a person reads and approves every one before it goes out. Use this when a wrong answer would embarrass you or cost real money. Almost every good first project starts here.
- **Act with approval.** AI proposes an action and does it only after you say yes. Use this when the task is repetitive enough to hand off but the stakes still warrant a check on each run. (This is the ground Module 05 covers.)
- **Fully automatic.** AI runs the task end to end with no human in the loop. Reserve this for low mistake-cost, well-proven tasks — the ones where a slip is cheap and rare, and you've watched it behave for a while first.

The skill is matching the rung to the mistake-cost. The consultancy's Friday note is fine as **draft-for-a-human** — the partner reads it anyway. You would *not* jump it straight to fully automatic and let unreviewed summaries reach clients. Same task, and the mistake-cost tells you which rung is safe.

## Quick wins this month vs transformation this year

Two scales of ambition, and confusing them is a classic mistake.

- **A quick win** is something you can stand up this month: drafting, summarising, first-pass answers — with a person still checking. Low risk, fast payback, and it teaches you how AI behaves on your real work. The cafe's order sheet and the consultancy's Friday note are quick wins.
- **A transformation** is a whole-year ambition: entire workflows running with checkpoints, several steps chained together, less human touch over time. The e-commerce shop's dream of an order-to-reply-to-report system that mostly runs itself is a transformation. It is real and reachable — it is what Modules 04 and 05 build toward — but it is not where you *start*.

Start with a quick win. Quick wins compound: each one frees a little attention and teaches you something, and a few of them chained together *is* the transformation. Trying to leap straight to the transformation is how projects stall.

## What NOT to start with

The honest anti-hype list. Avoid these as first projects, no matter how tempting the demo looked:

- **Anything where a wrong answer is expensive or legal.** Contracts, pricing decisions, hiring calls, tax and compliance judgements. Not "never" — but never *first*, and never without a human firmly in the loop.
- **Anything you do twice a year.** It fails the frequency test; you'll spend more time setting it up than you'll ever save.
- **Anything you'd be embarrassed to have a customer discover was automated.** A heartfelt apology to an upset customer, a condolence note, a personal thank-you to your best client. If the value *is* the human touch, keep it human.

## Two more traps: broken processes and boiling the ocean

Two mistakes sink first projects even when the candidate was well chosen:

1. **Automating a broken process.** If a task is a mess — unclear steps, missing information, no one sure how it's really done — automating it just gives you **faster chaos**. Fix the process first: write down the steps, agree what 'good' looks like, *then* bring in AI. AI amplifies whatever process you point it at, good or bad.
2. **Boiling the ocean.** Trying to automate everything at once guarantees you finish nothing. Pick **one** task. Ship it. Learn from it. Then pick the next.

## Worked example: the agency's proposals

The **marketing agency** drafts client proposals constantly — high frequency, high pain. Mistake-cost is medium: a weak proposal loses a pitch, but it isn't legally dangerous, and an account lead reads every proposal before it's sent. So the right deployment is **draft-for-a-human**: AI produces a strong first draft from the brief, the account lead edits and approves, and it goes out. That is a quick win, this month. The agency's *bigger* vision — a system that watches for new briefs, drafts proposals, and files them for review automatically — is a transformation for later, once the simple drafting habit is proven and the proposal process itself is tidy and consistent. One task, deployed at the right rung, with the process fixed first. That is the whole method.

## What this buys you

You can now take any candidate from your map and say three things about it: is it a quick win or a transformation, which trust-rung fits its mistake-cost, and is the underlying process tidy enough to automate yet. That's everything you need to choose your one starting task — which is exactly what the hands-on step has you do.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A task scores HIGH on mistake-cost. According to the module, what does that tell you?

options.text: How to deploy it — lean toward draft-for-a-human or act-with-approval so a person checks the work — not whether to use AI at all
That AI should never be used for this task under any circumstances
That the task should be run fully automatically to remove human error
That the task is automatically your best first project

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Mistake-cost decides the how, not the whether: a high cost pushes you toward keeping a human in the loop (draft-for-a-human or act-with-approval), not toward abandoning AI. Option b is the trap the module explicitly rejects — high stakes mean 'carefully,' not 'never.' Option c is backwards: high mistake-cost is the last profile you'd run fully automatically. Option d confuses the axes — a strong first project is low mistake-cost (plus high frequency and pain), so a high-cost task is not automatically the best starting point.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An owner wants to automate a task, but nobody can agree on how the task is actually done today — the steps are unclear and information goes missing. What does the module advise?

options.text: Fix the process first — write down the steps and agree what good looks like — because automating a broken process just gives you faster chaos
Automate it immediately; AI will work out the missing steps and impose order on its own
Automate this task plus every other messy task at the same time to fix them all together
Abandon AI entirely, because unclear processes mean AI can never help this business

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The fix-the-process-first rule is exactly this situation: tidy the process before you automate, or you scale the mess. Option b is the mistake the rule warns against — AI amplifies whatever process you point it at, so it makes chaos faster, not cleaner. Option c compounds two traps at once (unfixed process plus boiling the ocean). Option d over-corrects; the advice is to fix the process, not to give up — once the steps are clear, this task may be a fine candidate.

questions.stem: Which task best fits the definition of 'repetitive knowledge work'?

options.text: Drafting the same kind of customer-onboarding email several times a week from a rough template
Choosing which new market to expand into next year
Physically restocking the shelves each morning
Coming up with an original brand name for a new product line

questions.explanation: The onboarding email is frequent, follows a pattern, and is made of words — all three checks pass, so it's repetitive knowledge work. The market-expansion choice is a rare, one-off strategic judgement with no repeatable pattern. Restocking shelves is physical work, not knowledge work (its material isn't words or numbers). Inventing an original brand name is creative and one-off, not a repeating pattern — so it fails the test too.

questions.stem: The module lists four areas to walk when hunting for AI candidates. Which set names them?

options.text: Getting customers, serving customers, running the place, the numbers
Hardware, software, networking, security
Hiring, firing, promoting, training
Short-term, medium-term, long-term, strategic

questions.explanation: The four areas are getting customers (sales/marketing), serving customers (support/delivery), running the place (operations/admin), and the numbers (finance/reporting) — a plain-language walk through any business. Option b is an IT-infrastructure list, unrelated to the business-function map. Option c covers only HR activities, which is one slice of one area, not the whole map. Option d is a time-horizon framing, not the functional areas the module uses.

questions.stem: What is the correct role of the mistake-cost axis when you score a candidate?

options.text: It decides how you deploy AI on the task (draft-for-a-human, act-with-approval, or fully automatic), not whether to use AI
It decides whether the task is allowed to use AI at all — high cost means no AI
It measures how often the task happens
It is the only axis that matters, so frequency and pain can be ignored

questions.explanation: Mistake-cost sets the deployment method: the higher the cost of a wrong answer, the more you keep a human in the loop. Option b is the exact misconception the module corrects — high stakes change the how, not the whether. Option c confuses mistake-cost with frequency, which is a separate axis. Option d is wrong because all three axes matter together; mistake-cost tells you how to deploy, while frequency and pain tell you whether it's worth prioritising.

questions.stem: Which is the clearest example of a 'quick win' rather than a 'transformation'?

options.text: Having AI draft first-pass replies to product reviews for a person to approve, starting this month
Building a system where orders, replies, and reports flow between agents mostly untouched by humans
Re-platforming the entire business onto a new set of AI-run workflows over the coming year
Replacing the customer-service team with a fully autonomous agent handling every case end to end

questions.explanation: Drafting review replies for a human to approve is small, low-risk, and shippable this month — the definition of a quick win. Option b describes chained workflows running with little human touch, which is a transformation (Modules 04 and 05 territory). Option c is an explicitly year-long, whole-business change — the opposite of a quick win. Option d is a fully automatic, high-stakes replacement, which is both a transformation and a poor place to start.

questions.stem: Which task should you specifically NOT choose as a first AI project?

options.text: Deciding the final terms of a five-year commercial lease
Summarising this week's meeting notes into an action list for a person to review
Drafting routine social posts from a rough brief
Turning weekly supplier emails into an order sheet the owner checks

questions.explanation: A five-year lease decision is a rare, high-stakes, legally weighty one-off — it fails the frequency test and carries a ruinous mistake-cost, so it's the wrong first project. The other three are all strong first candidates: summarising meeting notes, drafting social posts, and building an order sheet are frequent, patterned, word-and-number tasks with a human check and low mistake-cost.

questions.stem: 'Automating a broken process just gives you faster chaos.' What does this rule tell you to do?

options.text: Tidy the process first — clarify the steps and agree what good looks like — before you automate it
Automate the broken process anyway, since speed is more valuable than order
Only ever automate processes that are already fully automated
Avoid automation forever if a process has ever been messy

questions.explanation: The rule is fix-the-process-first: AI amplifies whatever it's pointed at, so a messy process must be cleaned up before automation or the mess just scales. Option b ignores the warning — faster chaos is still chaos. Option c is nonsensical (a fully automated process needs no further automation). Option d over-reads the rule; the advice is to fix the process, not to abandon automation permanently.

questions.stem: The consultancy's Friday status note scored LOW on mistake-cost because a partner reviews it before it reaches the client. If that review step were removed, what changes?

options.text: The mistake-cost rises, because an unreviewed error now reaches the client directly — so the safe deployment rung and the task's ranking both change
Nothing — mistake-cost is a fixed property of the task and never depends on whether a human checks the output
The mistake-cost falls, because removing a step always makes a task safer
The frequency rises, because the note now goes out faster

questions.explanation: The low score depended on the human check catching errors; remove it and a mistake now lands on the client, so mistake-cost rises and you'd need a safer deployment (keep a human in the loop). Option b is wrong because mistake-cost is about consequences in context, and the review step is part of that context. Option c is backwards — removing the safety check raises risk, it doesn't lower it. Option d confuses axes: removing a review affects mistake-cost, not how often the task occurs.

questions.stem: You've mapped eight promising candidates across the four areas. What does the module tell you to do next?

options.text: Score them on frequency, pain, and mistake-cost, then pick ONE to start with
Start automating all eight at once so you make progress everywhere
Pick the single most emotionally painful one regardless of the other axes
Choose the one with the highest mistake-cost, since that's where AI adds the most value

questions.explanation: The method is to score all candidates and commit to one starting task — ranking beats guessing, and one shipped project beats eight stalled ones. Option b is 'boiling the ocean,' which the module warns guarantees you finish nothing. Option c ignores two of the three axes; pain alone doesn't make a good first project (a rare or high-stakes task can be very painful yet a poor start). Option d inverts the guidance — a high mistake-cost task is a riskier, not better, first project.

scenarios.title: The e-commerce inbox: start small, or build the system?
scenarios.tagline: The judgment call from the module's debate — quick win this week versus transformation this year, on one real inbox.
scenarios.context: You advise the e-commerce shop from the recurring cast. Its customer-service inbox is overwhelmed: dozens of 'where's my order?' messages a day, plus product reviews to reply to, plus the occasional genuinely upset customer. The owner has read about AI 'transforming' support and wants to automate the whole inbox. Your job is to steer them to the right first move without either over-promising or dismissing the ambition. Walk the decision with them.

steps.prompt: The owner opens with: 'Let's put AI on the entire inbox — every message, answered automatically, no staff needed.' What's your first move?

choices.text: Slow it down and split the inbox by task and mistake-cost — the repetitive 'where's my order?' questions are one candidate, review replies another, upset-customer messages a third — and score each before automating anything
choices.rationale: This is the method: don't treat 'the inbox' as one thing. Splitting by task and mistake-cost reveals that routine order-status questions are a great first candidate while sensitive messages are not — which is exactly what stops the owner over-automating.

choices.text: Agree, but insist that everything stays as draft-for-a-human so a person approves every single reply before it sends
choices.rationale: The human-in-the-loop instinct is right and safe, but blanket-drafting the whole inbox misses the real insight: different messages have very different mistake-costs, so they deserve different treatment. You'd be leaving easy wins slow and treating trivial and sensitive messages the same.

choices.text: Agree and switch the whole inbox to fully automatic replies to prove AI can handle it end to end
choices.rationale: Fully automatic across an entire inbox — including upset customers — is the highest-mistake-cost deployment on the least-suitable messages. This is exactly the over-reach the module warns against; one bad automated reply to a distressed customer is the kind of thing you'd be embarrassed to have discovered.

steps.prompt: You've singled out the 'where's my order?' questions as the first candidate. How should AI be deployed on them for a quick win this week?

choices.text: Draft-for-a-human: AI drafts the order-status reply, a staff member glances at it and approves before it sends — a quick win with a human check while trust is built
choices.rationale: High frequency, high pain, and — with a quick human glance — low mistake-cost: the ideal quick-win profile, deployed at the draft-for-a-human rung. It ships this week and teaches the owner how AI behaves on their real messages.

choices.text: Fully automatic from day one: these questions are simple and repetitive, so let AI answer them with no human check at all
choices.rationale: Order-status questions are genuinely low-stakes, so this isn't reckless — but jumping straight to no human check on day one skips the 'watch it behave for a while first' step. Start with a check, then relax it once it's proven; that's the safer path to the same place.

choices.text: Don't automate these at all — save AI for the upset-customer messages, since that's where staff struggle most
choices.rationale: This inverts the mistake-cost logic. Upset-customer messages are where the human touch matters most and the cost of a wrong answer is highest — the worst first candidate, not the best. The routine questions are where the quick win lives.

steps.prompt: The quick win is live and saving real time. The owner now asks about their bigger dream: order → auto-reply → weekly report, all chained together with little human touch. How do you frame it?

choices.text: As a transformation for later (Modules 04–05): reachable by chaining proven quick wins with checkpoints — but only after each piece works on its own and the underlying processes are tidy
choices.rationale: This lands the debate honestly: the transformation is real and reachable, but it's built from compounding quick wins with checkpoints, not leapt to. It also carries the fix-the-process-first rule into the bigger build.

choices.text: As something to start building right now in parallel, wiring all three steps together before the first quick win has really settled
choices.rationale: The ambition is correct and the direction is right, but building the full chain before the first piece has settled risks boiling the ocean and automating steps whose processes aren't yet proven. Sequence it: prove each piece, then chain.

choices.text: As hype to forget — small drafting help is all AI will ever realistically do for a business this size
choices.rationale: This is the cynical over-correction the debate explicitly rejects. Quick wins compound into systems; dismissing the transformation entirely is as wrong as leaping straight to it. The honest answer is 'yes, later, built from proven pieces.'

labs.title: Build your AI opportunity map — with the AI's help
labs.goal: Produce your signature artifact for this module: a scored AI opportunity map of your own business, with three candidates circled and one chosen as your starting task. You'll build it by interviewing yourself function-by-function in Automatos chat, then arguing with the AI to sharpen it.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

You'll use the free Automatos platform's chat to run a structured interview on your own business and come out with a ranked map. Everything here happens in ordinary chat — you don't need to build agents, connect tools, or upload anything yet.

### Before you start

- **Sign in.** A personal workspace is created for you automatically on first login, and you're the admin of it — so every step here is achievable solo. (See the platform's Welcome / Quick start.)
- **Open Chat.** The platform automatically routes your message to the best-suited agent — its Universal Router reads what you send and picks the right worker, so you don't have to choose one. Just type.

### Run the interview

1. **Describe your business in about five sentences.** What you sell, who to, how many people, and the handful of tasks that eat your week. Paste it into chat.
2. **Ask the AI to interview you, one area at a time.** Prompt it roughly: *"Interview me function by function — getting customers, serving customers, running the place, the numbers. In each, ask me what repetitive work made of words or numbers I do more than weekly. Don't move on until we've found the candidates in that area."* Answer honestly; let it dig.
3. **Ask it to build the scored table.** Prompt: *"Now put every candidate in a table scored low/medium/high on three axes — how often (frequency), how painful (pain), and how bad a wrong answer would be (mistake-cost). Add a column suggesting how to deploy each: draft-for-a-human, act-with-approval, or fully automatic — based on its mistake-cost."*
4. **Argue with it — this is the important part.** It will get some things wrong: over-rating a rare task, under-rating a mistake-cost, suggesting fully-automatic where a human should check. Push back in plain language and make it re-score. You know your business; it doesn't. Cut what it got wrong.
5. **Circle three, choose one.** Narrow to your three strongest candidates (high frequency, high pain, low mistake-cost), then pick the single one you'll start with. Sanity-check your choice against the module: is it a quick win (not a year-long transformation)? Is the underlying process tidy enough to automate, or does it need fixing first?
6. **Save the result off the platform.** Copy the final table into a document you keep — a note, a doc, a spreadsheet. This is your artifact. (You can later upload it to a Knowledge Base in Module 03 so your agents can reference it, but for now just keep it somewhere safe.)

### Why it matters

This map is the backbone of the whole track. It's your Module 08 capstone input, and the one task you circled becomes the workflow you automate in Module 04. Building it *with* the AI also gives you a first honest feel for what it's good at (structuring, summarising, drafting) and where you have to overrule it (judgement about your own business) — which is the real lesson of the module.

### A note on cost

Running chats uses an AI model, and models cost money per use. The realistic model for an operator is bring-your-own-key — you supply your own provider key in Settings and pay the provider directly. This interview is short and cheap, but it's worth knowing now; Module 07 covers cost properly.
checklist: Signed in and confirmed you're working in your own personal workspace
Described your business to the AI in chat and let it interview you across all four areas (getting customers, serving customers, running the place, the numbers)
Produced a table of candidate tasks scored low/medium/high on frequency, pain, and mistake-cost
Each candidate has a suggested deployment (draft-for-a-human / act-with-approval / fully automatic) matched to its mistake-cost
Argued with at least one of the AI's scores or suggestions and made it re-score
Three candidates circled and ONE chosen as your starting task, sanity-checked as a quick win with a tidy-enough process
ARTIFACT SAVED: your AI opportunity map, kept in a document for Modules 04 and 08

resources.title: Google Generative AI Leader — exam guide (business-strategy section)
resources.annotation: A vendor-light, business-toned guide. For this module, read the 'Business strategies for a successful gen AI solution' material: how to spot where generative AI genuinely fits, the difference between quick wins and larger transformations, and why fit-to-task (not hype) drives adoption. Skip the vendor-product specifics — you want the plain business framing.

resources.title: Automatos AI — About (platform docs)
resources.annotation: The plain-English description of what the platform is: agents are AI workers (not chatbots), the Universal Router sends each message to the best worker automatically, and you upload documents so agents get RAG-powered access to your data. Read it to understand what 'doing work on the platform' actually means before the hands-on step.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Welcome & Quick start (platform docs)
resources.annotation: The quick-start you'll follow for the lab: sign in (a personal workspace is created automatically on first login), then open Chat and type — the platform routes your message to the right agent. This is the 'free platform' entry point for every hands-on step in the track.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Activity command centre (platform docs)
resources.annotation: A look at what 'running knowledge work' looks like once it's on the platform: a dashboard of what agents are doing now, scheduled routines, reports they file, and long-running missions. Useful context for picturing where a candidate task from your map would eventually live — you don't need to do anything here yet.

resources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (overview)
resources.annotation: An external, vendor-neutral catalogue of the main ways AI language systems go wrong. You don't need the technical detail here — skim it only to reinforce the module's honest point that AI has real failure modes, which is exactly why mistake-cost decides how carefully you deploy. It's covered properly in Module 06 (risk and safety).

resources.title: EU AI Act — implementation timeline (orientation only)
resources.annotation: Orientation, not legal advice. The stable frame to take from it: AI-specific law is phasing in over the next few years, most small-business uses sit in the lower-risk categories, and existing data-protection law already applies to what you do. The exact dates are still in flux, so treat this as a sense of direction — Module 06 returns to it, and the AIGP track goes deep for anyone who needs it.
